summary: Gruber highlights a useful but little-known feature in Mac OS X Jaguar's system-wide Script menu: creating an Applications subfolder inside ~/Library/Scripts/ allows application-specific scripts to appear at the top level of the Script menu when that app is frontmost. He credits Erik Barzeski for discovering the trick and shares it with his readers. He also takes a jab at Barzeski for breaking all his site's URLs during a reorganization, linking to Tim Berners-Lee's guidance on permanent URIs. Finally, he agrees with Michael Tsai's criticism of the Script menu's use of disabled menu items as labels.
key_points: Jaguar's Script menu supports application-specific scripts via a ~/Library/Scripts/Applications/<AppName>/ folder structure
Barzeski broke all his site's links during a reorganization, violating the principle that cool URIs don't change
The Script menu's use of disabled menu items as section labels is a UI antipattern seen across several Apple apps
money_quotes: Note to Erik from Tim Berners-Lee: Don't do that.
Unfortunately, Script Menu uses the annoying "disabled menu item as a label" trick that's also seen in the Apple menu, iTunes, Mail, and Safari.
key_insight: Mac OS X's Script menu has a hidden but powerful feature for organizing application-specific scripts, though its UI implementation leaves something to be desired.
